content:
We label the 3×3 grid by coordinates (i,j), 1≤i,j≤3.  Two squares are adjacent iff they share an edge.  Since every edge‐adjacency must join an odd to an even number, the parity‐pattern on the board must be a proper 2‐colouring of the 3×3 grid graph.  But the grid is bipartite with parts of sizes 5 and 4, and we have 5 odd numbers and 4 even numbers.  Hence the odds must occupy exactly the 5‐cell part and the evens the 4‐cell part.  Up to parity there is only one choice of which “colour” of the checkerboard gets the odds (namely the part of size 5).

Thus the total number of raw Isthmian fillings is
   5! ways to place the five odd numbers in the odd‐cells
 × 4! ways to place the four even numbers in the even‐cells
 = 120·24 = 2880.

Finally we quotient out by rotations of the square.  By Burnside’s Lemma one checks that no nontrivial rotation can fix a bijective numbering, so all 2880 fillings fall into orbits of size 4.  Hence the number of distinct arrangements up to rotation is

(1/4)·2880 = 720.

Answer: \(\boxed{720}\).
